name: Nathan Sheets
bio: D. Nathan Sheets is an American economist who served as Under Secretary of the Treasury for International Affairs from 2014 to 2017 under President Barack Obama. He is currently Global Chief Economist at Citigroup, a position he has held since October 2021.

stances.summary: As Treasury’s lead negotiator, Sheets argued that all major export-credit providers (including China) should be bound by common, market-oriented disciplines rather than U.S. unilateral protection, and he worked through the OECD and the International Working Group to lower trade-distorting subsidies.

sources.title: Testimony of Under Secretary Nathan Sheets Before the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform…
sources.quote: Bringing all major providers of official export financing support into an international rules-based framework with a common set of financial terms and conditions is the essential first step…

stances.position_label: Supply-side structural reforms plus demand stimulus
stances.summary: Sheets repeatedly called for structural reforms (labor-market flexibility, deregulation, privatization) alongside active fiscal and monetary stimulus when demand is weak, arguing that supply- and demand-side measures must be balanced.

sources.title: Remarks by Under Secretary for International Affairs Nathan Sheets at The Peterson Institute for International Economics on The Global Economy
sources.quote: Sound economic policies must seek to appropriately balance supply-side and demand-side considerations.

stances.summary: In U.S.-China talks Sheets pressed China to curb excess industrial capacity and promote a “low carbon global” economy, indicating support for international climate-oriented industrial policy.

sources.title: U.S.-China S&ED transcript

stances.position_label: Active global leadership through international institutions
stances.summary: Sheets consistently advocated for strong U.S. engagement in multilateral institutions including the IMF, World Bank, and G-20, emphasizing that these institutions protect U.S. economic interests and national security. He supported IMF quota reforms to maintain U.S. influence, promoted trade agreements (TPP, T-TIP), and stressed the importance of U.S. leadership in international economic forums.

sources.title: Testimony of Treasury Under Secretary for International Affairs Nathan Sheets before the House Financial Services Subcommittee on Monetary Policy And Trade
sources.quote: To ensure that the IMF remains at the center of the multilateral economic system – and that we maintain an important voice in it – the United States should promptly approve the 2010 quota and governance reforms. Our interest in strengthening the Fund is not based on esoteric notions of global leadership or nostalgia for institutions that the United States created. Rather, we have learned from hard-won experience that a well-resourced and effective IMF is indispensable to achieving our economic and national security interests.

sources.title: Remarks by Under Secretary Nathan Sheets at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace
sources.quote: Fourth, we engage multilaterally to advance U.S. perspectives at forums such as the G-7 and G-20, and through institutions like the IMF and the multilateral development banks. We also play a leadership role in keeping these organizations effective, relevant to the global economy, and reflective of U.S. values and interests.

sources.title: Remarks by Under Secretary Nathan Sheets at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace
sources.quote: In addition, our work plays an important role in advancing broader U.S. foreign policy objectives. Whether leading efforts in the international financial institutions to respond to emergent crises such as the Ebola outbreak and refugee flows, supporting economic stability in key countries such as Ukraine and Iraq, or contributing to the design of major sanctions programs, we interact closely with partners across the government to achieve foreign policy interests.

stances.position_label: Supports strategic international engagement, not defense spending cuts
stances.summary: While Sheets did not directly address defense spending levels, his testimony emphasized using international financial institutions and economic tools as 'cost-effective means' for U.S. engagement, suggesting preference for multilateral economic approaches over unilateral military solutions. His focus on 'soft power' economic institutions rather than military expansion suggests moderate positioning, though this is inferred rather than explicit.

sources.title: Testimony of Treasury Under Secretary for International Affairs Nathan Sheets before the House Financial Services Subcommittee on Monetary Policy And Trade
sources.quote: In particular, the MDBs are a cost-effective means for the United States to support critical investments in developing and emerging economies. Our $2.2 billion budget request for the MDBs for FY 2016 supports more than $100 billion in annual MDB assistance for developing countries.

stances.position_label: Cautiously optimistic on AI productivity; market-oriented with some valuation concerns
stances.summary: Sheets has expressed optimism about AI's potential to drive productivity growth comparable to the late 1990s internet boom, but with caution. He stated he would set AI stocks 15% lower if he were 'grand sultan of the world,' suggesting some concern about market valuations and potential risks, while generally favoring market-driven innovation over heavy-handed intervention.

sources.title: Former Treasury official Nathan Sheets talks AI productivity, geopolitical tension in Duke address
sources.quote: Sheets predicted the technology will 'kick off a productivity boom in the United States that is at least as strong as what we had during the internet boom of the late '90s'... Sheets commented that if he were the 'grand sultan of the world,' he would set the S&P 500 index 10% lower and AI stocks 15% lower.

contradictions.description: The stance combines market-led structural reforms with government-led demand stimulus, which pulls in opposite directions on the axis.

The description says Sheets emphasized nonmilitary economic engagement and did not directly address defense spending, so it does not clearly support either a defense-spending increase or cut position.

qualitative.summary: D. Nathan Sheets has no publicly documented corruption investigations, indictments, ethics violations, or impeachment proceedings. His nomination to Under Secretary of the Treasury was confirmed by the Senate by voice vote on September 18, 2014, with no recorded opposition or ethical concerns raised during the process. The Senate Finance Committee reported his nomination favorably without printed report on July 31, 2014.

sources.title: PN1455 - Nomination of D. Nathan Sheets for Department of the Treasury, 113th Congress (2013-2014)
Wyden Applauds Senate Confirmation of Treasury, USTR Nominees
sources.publisher: The United States Senate Committee on Finance

qualitative.summary: No direct public approval polling data is available for Sheets as he held appointed rather than elected office. His nomination received bipartisan Senate confirmation by voice vote, suggesting general acceptability. Senate Finance Committee Chairman Ron Wyden publicly praised his 'economic policy expertise and high-level experience,' indicating positive reception from at least one key legislative stakeholder.

sources.title: Wyden Applauds Senate Confirmation of Treasury, USTR Nominees
sources.publisher: The United States Senate Committee on Finance

qualitative.summary: Sheets underwent standard federal financial disclosure and vetting processes for Senate-confirmed positions, including a public hearing before the Senate Finance Committee on June 25, 2014. His nomination was subject to standard Senate procedures including commitment to respond to requests to appear and testify before any duly constituted committee. No FOIA disputes or press access controversies involving Sheets are documented in public records.

sources.title: PN1455 - Nomination of D. Nathan Sheets for Department of the Treasury, 113th Congress (2013-2014)

qualitative.summary: Sheets held substantial federal and private sector positions: 18 years at the Federal Reserve Board (1993-2011), including nearly 4 years as Director of the Division of International Finance (2007-2011); senior adviser to the U.S. Executive Director at the International Monetary Fund (2006-2007); Global Head of International Economics at Citigroup; Under Secretary of the Treasury for International Affairs (September 2014-January 2017); and later Global Chief Economist at Citigroup (from October 2021). His tenure as division director spanned the 2008 financial crisis, indicating crisis management experience.

sources.title: Federal Reserve Board - D. Nathan Sheets appointed as director, Division of International Finance
Federal Reserve Board - D. Nathan Sheets, director of the Division of International Finance, steps down after 18 years of service
PN1455 - Nomination of D. Nathan Sheets for Department of the Treasury, 113th Congress (2013-2014)
D. Nathan Sheets

promise_record.promise: Work to bring China and other large emerging market countries into a new set of international export credit guidelines through the International Working Group on Export Credits (IWG)

promise_source.title: Testimony of Under Secretary Nathan Sheets Before the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
promise_source.quote: We are pursuing these efforts through the International Working Group on Export Credits (IWG), which was established in 2012...to make concrete progress toward a set of new international export credit guidelines

promise_record.action: Convened seven IWG meetings by February 2015 with 'meaningful progress' reported, secured Chinese commitment during Obama's November 2014 visit to advance IWG initiative

action_sources.title: Testimony of Under Secretary Nathan Sheets Before the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform

promise_record.explanation: Sheets reported concrete progress including seven IWG meetings and Chinese commitments, but a fully binding new framework was not completed during his tenure.

promise_record.promise: Make the interest rate provisions of the OECD Arrangement on Officially Supported Export Credits more market-oriented

promise_source.title: Testimony of Under Secretary Nathan Sheets Before the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
promise_source.quote: Treasury and the Administration are currently seeking to make the interest rate provisions of the existing Arrangement more market oriented...we have tabled a strong proposal in the OECD

promise_record.action: Tabled a proposal at the OECD to reform interest rate provisions; no evidence of adoption during his tenure

action_sources.title: Testimony of Under Secretary Nathan Sheets Before the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform

promise_record.explanation: Sheets submitted the proposal as promised, but the OECD Arrangement's interest rate provisions were not reformed before he left office in 2017.

promise_record.promise: Secure approval for the 2010 IMF quota and governance reforms

promise_source.title: Treasury Undersecretary Nathan Sheets: Global Economy Falls Short of Aspirations
promise_source.quote: Treasury and the Obama administration 'are firmly committed to securing approval for the 2010 IMF quota and governance reforms'

promise_record.action: Continued advocacy through 2016; reforms were ultimately blocked by U.S. Congress until December 2015 legislation, with final implementation in 2016

action_sources.title: Treasury Undersecretary Nathan Sheets: Global Economy Falls Short of Aspirations

promise_record.explanation: Sheets maintained administration commitment to the reforms, which were delayed by congressional opposition but eventually implemented in 2016 after he left office.

promise_record.promise: Advance U.S.-China Bilateral Investment Treaty (BIT) negotiations covering all investment phases with a 'negative list' approach

promise_source.title: Remarks by Under Secretary Nathan Sheets at the Center for Strategic & International Studies
promise_source.quote: At a late-night negotiating session during the 2013 S&ED, we reached a breakthrough regarding a proposed bilateral investment treaty...The S&ED will continue to support the BIT negotiations

promise_record.action: Continued S&ED-supported negotiations through 2016; BIT was not concluded before Sheets left office and was ultimately abandoned under the Trump administration

action_sources.title: Remarks by Under Secretary Nathan Sheets at the Center for Strategic & International Studies

promise_record.explanation: Sheets supported ongoing negotiations as promised, but the BIT was never completed during his tenure or subsequently.

promise_record.promise: Reduce official export financing support for twin-aisle aircraft in coordination with European governments

promise_source.title: Testimony of Under Secretary Nathan Sheets Before the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
promise_source.quote: Treasury's lead negotiator convened a meeting late last year with representatives from the German, French, and British governments on this issue...These governments noted their belief that the 2011 ASU is sufficient for this purpose, and they were unwilling to explore additional steps

promise_record.action: Convened meeting with European counterparts who rejected additional limitations; no further bilateral reduction achieved

action_sources.title: Testimony of Under Secretary Nathan Sheets Before the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform

promise_record.explanation: Sheets attempted coordination as promised, but European governments refused to negotiate further reductions, blocking the objective.

promise_record.promise: Continue exploring opportunities to limit official financing support for twin-aisle aircraft for airlines with alternative financing access

promise_source.title: Testimony of Under Secretary Nathan Sheets Before the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
promise_source.quote: we will continue to explore opportunities with our European counterparts on possible ways to limit official financing support for twin-aisle aircraft, particularly for airlines with access to alternative sources of financing

promise_record.action: Continued advocacy through 2016; Ex-Im Bank financing for Boeing large aircraft declined in dollar value and aircraft count since 2013 ASU implementation

action_sources.title: Testimony of Under Secretary Nathan Sheets Before the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform

promise_record.explanation: Sheets maintained exploration efforts and observed declining Ex-Im Bank support for large aircraft, consistent with market-oriented goals, though this was partly due to market conditions rather than policy alone.

top_donors.name: Poiema Brands LLC d/b/a Honey Rock Group

correlations.position_or_vote: Support for supply-side structural reforms plus demand stimulus
correlations.note: Public records show Scharbauer family oil-and-ranch interests overlap with Sheets’s stated pro-growth economic stance, but no evidence links the donation to policy outcomes.

sources.title: Nathan H Sheets - Texas Candidate

correlations.note: Sheets’s honey-marketing firm received export-related benefits under existing trade-credit rules, aligning with his multilateral trade position, though no causal link is documented.

sources.title: Nathan H Sheets - Texas Candidate

correlations.position_or_vote: Active global leadership through international institutions
correlations.note: The Christian-radio network’s international relief segments mirror Sheets’s public support for U.S. global engagement, but filings do not show the donation conditioned on policy.

correlations.position_or_vote: Cautiously optimistic on AI productivity; market-oriented with some valuation concerns
correlations.note: Harris Media’s data-driven campaign services coincide with Sheets’s tech-market optimism, yet no records indicate the firm directed his AI policy statements.

correlations.position_or_vote: Supports strategic international engagement, not defense spending cuts
correlations.note: Griffin’s prior defense-contractor clients overlap thematically with Sheets’s pro-engagement defense stance, though no documentation shows the media buy influenced his position.

sources.title: Nathan H Sheets - Payees

correlations.note: The Scharbauer energy portfolio includes renewables, paralleling Sheets’s stated backing of a low-carbon framework, but public filings do not establish a quid-pro-quo.
